messages.content: How many credits do Webb students need to graduate?
Webb requires a minimum of 20 credits for graduation. [source: course catalog]
Does Webb still expect students to take five classes each semester?
Yes. The course catalog says all students are required to take five courses each semester. Many take a sixth, but seven is not allowed. [source: course catalog]
Can a student ask for a specific teacher during course changes?

Not as a normal basis for a change. Webb says students are scheduled by subject, not by teacher or time block, and requests for a particular teacher or free block will not be granted. [source: course catalog]
What is the deadline for returning students to finish course selection in SIS?
For the 2026-2027 catalog, the deadline for returning students is April 17. [source: course catalog]
When does Webb say students must submit the recommendation-level appeals form?
The catalog lists March 17 as the date students must submit the recommendation-level appeals form. [source: course catalog]
Does Webb encourage summer acceleration?
No. The catalog says Webb rarely allows students to skip a course through summer work and that enrichment, not acceleration, should usually be the goal of summer programs. [source: course catalog]
What is the deadline for a returning student to request a summer course for acceleration?
The course catalog says requests must be made in writing by April 15. [source: course catalog]
How much world language does Webb require, and what does it recommend for highly selective colleges?
Webb requires three years of the same language. It recommends four years for students aiming at highly selective colleges. [source: course catalog]
Does Webb count courses taken elsewhere into the Webb GPA?

Not generally. The handbook says only courses taken at Webb during the school year count in a student's Webb GPA, and the catalog says semester- or year-away program grades are sent with transcripts but not calculated into Webb GPA. [source: handbook] [source: course catalog]
Who is the right contact for attendance?
The handbook lists Steven Galarsa for attendance and gives the phone number (909) 447-7078 for unanticipated day-student absences. [source: handbook]
Can parents inspect school records?
Yes. The handbook says parents have the right to inspect their child's school records and should contact the registrar for more information. [source: handbook]
If a textbook is not returned, what happens?
The handbook says any unreturned books at the end of the year will be charged in full. [source: handbook]
Does Webb have a built-in health program for all four years?
Yes. The course catalog says Health & Living is a four-year health program designed around students' developmental milestones. [source: course catalog]
What is the academic reason Webb gives for small classes?

Webb ties small classes to individualized attention and support. The admissions page highlights a 6-to-1 student-to-teacher ratio and says students receive individualized attention. [source: admissions]
Can a student take Claremont Colleges classes whenever they want?

No. The catalog says this is only for students who have completed all advanced-level course offerings in a department and still need multiple approvals, including Webb and college approvals. [source: course catalog]
What does Webb say about Independent Study length?
Independent Study courses are typically no more than one-half credit and one semester in length. [source: course catalog]
Is participation in the afternoon program optional?
No. The catalog says all students participate in the afternoon program each season of the year. [source: course catalog]
How many seasons of team sport does Webb require each year?
At least one. The course catalog says all students must participate in an interscholastic sport at least one season each year. [source: course catalog]
Can winter Dance Company count toward the team-sport requirement?
Yes. The catalog says the winter dance program counts as a sports credit because of its physical demands and culminating performance. [source: course catalog]
Does Sports Broadcasting ever count for team sport credit?
Yes for juniors or seniors. The catalog says juniors or seniors can earn team sport credit through Sports Broadcasting. [source: course catalog]
Where do the golf teams practice?
The course catalog says the girls and boys golf teams practice at Marshall Canyon. [source: course catalog]
What equipment do students need for triathlon?
The catalog says students need running shoes, a bathing suit, and access to a bicycle. It also notes that at least one off-campus race is typically required. [source: course catalog]
What is special about Webb's paleontology opportunities?

Webb says students study paleontology in 9th grade, work with the Alf Museum on campus, and can continue through honors and advanced research. It also says the school has the only paleontology museum on a high school campus in the world. [source: alf museum]
Does Webb publish where its graduates commonly matriculate?

Yes. Webb publishes a top-40 matriculation list and a college guidance profile showing recent matriculation and longer-term enrollment patterns. [source: college guidance] [source: college guidance profile]
Does Webb have first-generation or low-income affinity support?
Yes. Webb lists FLIers as an affinity group for first-generation and low-income students. [source: clubs and affinity groups]
How should I decide between Honors Journalism and AdvSt Modern Media?

Start with the sequence itself. AdvSt Modern Media requires Honors Journalism, so the question is usually not which one to jump to first, but whether you are ready for the journalism foundation now and the advanced follow-up later. If you have not taken Journalism yet, Honors Journalism is the immediate step. [source: course catalog]
I like film, but I am not sure whether I want Filmmaking Studio I or Media Arts.

Pick based on what you want to make. Filmmaking Studio I is centered on video storytelling, camera work, lighting, audio, editing, and short-film production. Media Arts is broader and more digital, with illustration, animation, graphic design, game design, and 3D modeling. If film is the actual goal, Studio I is the better fit. [source: fine arts]
What is the real difference between standard challenge and appropriate challenge?

Appropriate challenge means the course is hard enough to stretch you but still realistic within your full life. A class can look impressive on paper and still be inappropriate if it overwhelms your schedule, weakens your performance elsewhere, or pushes you into constant recovery mode.
How should a student think about taking more than two advanced classes?

Webb's own guidance is cautious here. The catalog says many highly talented students still cannot reasonably manage more than two Honors or Advanced Studies classes. So the question is not just whether you can survive a heavier stack, but whether it is the wisest way to learn and perform well. [source: course catalog]
Can you explain the difference between Honors and Advanced Studies without making it sound like marketing?

Honors usually means a harder version of strong high-school-level work, with extra depth and more advanced content. Advanced Studies is Webb's more intensive top level, meant to feel more like high-level, research-aware, college-style work. The two are both rigorous, but they are not interchangeable.
Which is better for someone who wants to build a portfolio: Advanced Art or New Media Culture & Communication?

It depends on what kind of portfolio. Advanced Art fits students building a more traditional or mixed-media visual art path rooted in drawing, painting, observation, and developing artistic voice. New Media Culture & Communication fits students building a digital portfolio in areas like illustration, graphic design, animation, 3D, or game design. [source: fine arts]
How should I use office hours if I am embarrassed that I do not get something?

Go anyway, and go with one concrete point of confusion. Teachers can usually help much faster when you say, 'I understand the first part, but I lose the logic here,' instead of apologizing for not understanding. Office hours are a tool, not a confession.
What makes a course load balanced if you also care about sports and leadership?

A balanced load leaves room for your main classes, your main commitments, and sleep without every week turning into damage control. Webb's course-planning advice explicitly says non-academic commitments and leadership should be weighed alongside rigor, not treated as invisible. [source: course catalog]
I am deciding between Neuroscience and Honors Astrophysics. How should I choose?

Choose based on the kind of questions you want to live with. Neuroscience is better if you are drawn to biological systems, the brain, and behavior. Honors Astrophysics is better if you are pulled toward physics, space, and a more math-heavy style of thinking. The better class is the one whose daily thinking you actually want, not just the one with the cooler title.
What is a good way to judge whether a language class should stay standard or go honors?

Look at three things: whether the foundation is truly strong, whether you enjoy the language enough to want more of it, and whether the rest of your schedule can absorb the faster pace. Honors language is a bad choice if it becomes constant survival instead of real growth.
How should I think about choosing between Statistics and Honors Number Theory?

Statistics is usually the more practical choice if you want real-world data reasoning, research relevance, or application across fields. Honors Number Theory is better if you love abstract mathematical patterns and want math for its own ideas. One is broadly applied. The other is more conceptually pure.
How do I know whether a sixth class is actually worth adding?

It is worth adding if it clearly improves your education more than it harms your balance. If the reason is just 'it looks stronger,' that is usually weaker than people think. A sixth class should earn its place.
Can you explain what a free block is really for at a school like Webb?
A free block is not just empty time. It is built-in academic space for homework, teacher help, organization, and getting ahead before the evening gets crowded. Used well, it protects your nights.
How should a student choose between Theater Arts 1-2 and Honors Theater Performance and Production?

If you are new to theater or still building your foundation, Theater Arts 1-2 is the better place to start. Honors Theater Performance and Production is the stronger choice if you already have the prerequisite background and want a more demanding mix of performance, production, and showcase work. [source: fine arts]
What is the difference between acceleration and enrichment in summer?

Acceleration is trying to skip ahead in a normal sequence. Enrichment is using summer to learn something valuable without forcing the next school-year placement upward. Webb explicitly leans toward enrichment over acceleration. [source: course catalog]
How should someone compare Technology and Innovation Seminar I with HMC/Webb Advanced Courses in Computer Science?

Technology and Innovation Seminar I is the gentler entry point if you want applied innovation work while keeping your math path going. HMC/Webb Advanced CS is the more direct advanced computer-science move. The choice comes down to whether you want exploration and buildup or a stronger immediate CS signal. [source: course catalog]
What makes an honors appeal sound thoughtful instead of just ambitious?
A thoughtful appeal explains why the course is a fit, what evidence supports that, and how the student will manage the workload. It sounds weaker when it is mostly about status or proving something.
How should a student decide whether to keep four years of language?

Keep it if the language still adds real value, either because you enjoy it, you are strong in it, or it supports your broader goals. If it has become the least meaningful part of the schedule and crowds out something more important, then four years is not automatically the better answer.
What is the best way to approach required office hours if you get assigned them?

Treat them as structure, not punishment. Go in with the specific work or concept you need help with, use the time fully, and show your advisor and teacher that you are actually responding to the support instead of just showing up.
Is Girls Golf definitely still coached by Geoffrey Owers next fall?
I can't confirm that from the information I have. The athletics page shows current information, but future staffing can change, so I would check the latest official athletics listing.
What dorm room would I get if I enroll this year?
I can't confirm that from the information I have. Room assignments are student-specific and handled through school housing processes.
Did they already pick next year's Peer Advisors?
I can't confirm that from the information I have. That kind of decision should be checked through the actual leadership announcement or the appropriate school office.
What will the first Unbounded Days course list look like?

I can't confirm that from the information I have. Webb says the Unbounded Days catalogue is made available in the fall of the program year, so the exact offerings need to be checked there. [source: handbook]
What time is AI Club meeting tomorrow?
I can't confirm that from the information I have. Club meeting times are current logistics, so the safest place to check is the latest student communication.
Is Prom on campus this year?
I can't confirm that from the information I have. Event format and location can change year to year, so I would check the current calendar or student announcements.
Did my teacher already submit comments for the report?
I can't confirm that from the information I have. That would need to come from the teacher, advisor, or official student records system.
How many students are in my dorm next semester?
I can't confirm that from the information I have. Current dorm occupancy is a live administrative detail, not something I should guess about.
Will AdvSt Performance in Context actually run as planned?

I can't confirm that from the information I have. The fine arts page says it is planned for 2027-2028, but whether a course ultimately runs can still depend on scheduling and enrollment. [source: fine arts]
Who is definitely writing the strongest college rec for me?
I can't confirm that from the information I have. The better question is which teacher knows your work deeply and can write in detail about your thinking, growth, and character.
Is there a current SAT administration on campus next month?
I can't confirm that from the information I have. Webb says SAT administrations are offered periodically, but registration and availability are handled through the College Board. [source: handbook]
What are the odds they approve my accommodation request?
I can't confirm that from the information I have. Accommodation decisions are case-by-case and depend on the request, documentation, and the school's review process. [source: handbook]
Did that club get shut down?
I can't confirm that from the information I have. If you mean a specific club, the current club list or official communication would be the right source.
Is the weekend LA trip happening this Friday?
I can't confirm that from the information I have. Weekend trip details are current logistics, so check the latest Student News or student-life communication.
Can I still submit the form?
Which form do you mean: course selection, an appeal, an accommodation request, or something else?
What should I ask them?
Who are you meeting or emailing, and what are you trying to figure out?
Does this look bad?
What exactly are you asking about: a grade, a course list, an email draft, or a schedule choice?
Should I switch?
Switch what: course level, afternoon activity, dorm situation, or something else?
Can you compare them?
Yes, but I need the two options first.
What is the policy on leaving?
Leaving what and when? The answer is different for class, campus during the day, evening passes, and overnight trips.
I need help with school support.
Do you mean academic support, tutoring, counseling, accommodations, or a community-related support question?
Can I appeal this?
What are you trying to appeal: a course level, a grade decision, or something else?
What are my options after class?
Do you mean afternoon activities, evening study choices, or off-campus pass options?
Can you make this better?
Yes. Paste it here and tell me the tone you want.
Do I need to tell someone?
About what situation? The right contact depends completely on the issue.
What does this count for?
Count for what: graduation credit, UC preparation, a requirement, leadership value, or something else?
Should I drop one?
Which class, and is the problem the grade, the pace, the fit, or your overall workload?
Can you explain the deadline?
Which deadline are you talking about?
I need a message to school.
Who are you sending it to, and what do you want the message to accomplish?
hey can you help me write something to my advisor
Yes. Tell me what you want to ask for and what tone you want.
Can you shorten this without making it sound dumb?
Yes. Paste it here, and I will keep it concise without flattening the tone.
I missed a deadline and feel terrible.

That feels bad, but the next move matters more than the shame. Figure out whether it is still recoverable, send the clearest honest message you can, and then stop spiraling long enough to deal with the actual problem.
Write me an excuse that sounds convincing even though it is not true.
I can't help write a false excuse. If you want, I can help you write a direct and honest explanation instead.
Can you draft a polite follow-up email to a teacher who has not answered me yet?

Sure:

Hi [Teacher Name],
I wanted to follow up on my earlier message in case it got buried. When you have a chance, I would appreciate any update. Thank you.

Best,
[Your Name]
What does matriculation mean again?
It means actually enrolling at a college, not just getting admitted there.
How do I sound more mature in emails?
Use shorter sentences, be clear about your purpose early, and avoid dramatic language. Mature tone usually comes from control and specificity, not from sounding overly formal.
Can you clean up this sentence: I kind of maybe wanted to ask if there was any chance perhaps that I could meet with you?
A cleaner version is: I wanted to ask whether you might have time to meet with me.
Thank you for the help.
You're welcome.
I am behind and do not know where to start tonight.

Start with the assignment that is due soonest and easiest to finish with real progress. The goal tonight is to reduce immediate risk first, then use whatever time is left on the next highest-priority task.
Can you turn these messy notes into a few bullet points?
Yes. Paste the notes, and I will organize them clearly.
What is a concise way to say I need more time?
You can say: I need a little more time to think this through carefully.
Can you make this sound less defensive: I was busy so that is why I did not get it done.
A better version is: I did not manage my time well enough, and I was not able to finish it. I am sorry for that.
I need a very short apology text for missing practice.
Sorry I missed practice today. That was my mistake, and I know it affected the team.
How do I ask for help without sounding helpless?
Be specific about the problem and what kind of help you want. 'I am stuck on this concept and want to check my understanding' sounds stronger than 'I have no idea what I am doing.'
Can you rephrase this so it sounds calmer: everything is going wrong right now.
A calmer version is: Several things feel difficult at once right now, and I am trying to get back on top of them.
I want to sound appreciative but not fake in an email.
Keep it simple. One honest thank-you sentence is usually enough. Overdoing gratitude can make the message sound less real, not more.
I need to tell my teacher I am confused, but I do not want it to sound like I did nothing.
Try this: I worked through the assignment and understood part of it, but I am still confused about [specific concept]. Could I ask you a couple of questions about that?
I am choosing between a harder schedule and more sleep, and I hate that this is even a choice.
It is frustrating, but it is a real tradeoff. A schedule only helps if you can actually do it well. Sleep is not the opposite of seriousness.
So you would not automatically choose harder?
No. I would choose the strongest schedule that still lets you function like a real person, not the one that sounds best in isolation.
I found one document saying add/drop is ten academic days and another saying seven. Which do I trust?

Trust the version for the correct school year. The handbook for 2025-2026 says ten academic days, while the 2026-2027 course catalog says seven. When policies differ, the date matters. [source: handbook] [source: course catalog]
Use only confirmed school facts when answering policy questions. If the materials do not confirm something, say so plainly.
Does Webb excuse academic or extracurricular obligations for off-campus AP exams?
No. The course catalog says students who choose to sit for AP exams off campus will not be excused from Webb academic or extracurricular obligations. [source: course catalog]
I got recommended for several advanced classes, and now I feel like I am supposed to take all of them.

Recommendation is not the same as obligation. Webb's own course-planning guidance says students should weigh total load and that many very capable students still should not stack too many advanced courses. [source: course catalog]
So saying no to one is not automatically wasting an opportunity?
Right. It can actually be the more mature choice if it leads to a stronger overall year.
What is the better story for college apps: doing the hardest math or building a more balanced schedule with leadership?

There is no single title that wins that comparison automatically. Harder math can be the right choice if it clearly fits your interests and you can do it well. A balanced schedule with real leadership can be stronger than one extra advanced class if it better reflects your actual strengths and commitments.
So fit matters more than pure maxing out?
Yes. Fit, coherence, and performance matter more than trying to turn every dial to maximum at once.
Can you help me draft an email asking whether outside tutoring might make sense, but without making it sound like I am trying to go around school support?

Yes:

Hi [Name],
I wanted to ask about the process for considering outside tutoring in [subject]. I am continuing to use school support, including [teacher help/office hours], but I wanted to ask whether additional support might be appropriate and, if so, what the proper next steps would be.

Best,
[Your Name]
When the user asks about rumors or live logistics, do not guess.
People are saying there might be a dorm reshuffle soon. Is that actually happening?
I can't confirm that from the information I have. I would not treat a rumor like that as fact unless there is an official residential-life communication.
I kind of want to write a really angry message to my group because they keep ghosting, but I also know that is probably dumb.
That instinct makes sense, but an angry message usually gives you less control, not more. A better message names the problem, what is still missing, and the next deadline.
Can you write that instead?

Sure:

Hey, I need to be direct that communication has dropped off and it is affecting the project. We still need [specific missing pieces] by [time]. If you cannot do your part, please tell me as soon as possible so we can make a realistic plan.
I am deciding between staying in a course that is maybe too high and dropping to a level that fits better, and I hate that both options feel like losing.
That is a real emotional trap. But the decision is not between winning and losing. It is between two different versions of progress.
How do I think about it more clearly?

Ask which option gives you the stronger overall semester: better learning, more stable habits, and a more sustainable schedule. The answer is often less about pride than about what leads to real growth.
Keep the tone calm and direct. Do not invent specifics beyond what the school materials confirm.
Does Webb frame the advisory program as academic only?
No. Webb's admissions materials describe the advisory program as supporting students socially, emotionally, and academically. [source: admissions]
My parent thinks more classes always equals better. I do not think that is true, but I need a good way to explain it.

A good way to put it is this: a schedule should be strong enough to show challenge, but not so overloaded that the quality of learning and performance drops. More is only better if the student can still do the work well.
Can you make that shorter?
Sure: More classes only help if they improve the student's education instead of weakening performance across the board.
Is it weird that I want a class because I actually like it, even if it is not the most strategic choice?
No. Strategy matters, but real interest matters too. A coherent schedule is usually stronger when at least some of the hard choices are also genuinely meaningful to the student.
I need a message asking whether a course change is possible, but I do not want to sound impulsive.

You could write:

Hi [Name],
I wanted to ask whether it might make sense to discuss a course change in [course]. I have been trying to assess the fit carefully, and I would appreciate your guidance on whether a change is worth considering and what the process would be.

Best,
[Your Name]
Can you tell me whether this is a good idea?
I can, but I need the actual plan first.
When a question is underspecified, ask one brief clarifying question instead of guessing.
What should I do after school?
Do you want something academic, active, creative, social, or just low-stress?
I want to ask for an exception, but I also want to sound like I understand the rule.
Lead by acknowledging the rule first, then explain the specific reason your situation may merit review. That sounds more responsible than acting like the rule should not apply to you.
Can you draft that?

Sure:

Hi [Name],
I understand the usual policy regarding [rule]. I wanted to ask whether there might be any room for an exception in my specific situation because [brief reason]. I understand if the answer is no, but I wanted to ask respectfully and provide context.

Best,
[Your Name]
I know one student who always seems to take the hardest path, and it makes me feel like I am underdoing it.

That comparison is usually misleading because you only see the outside of someone else's schedule, not the cost structure underneath it. The better question is whether your own choices are strong, coherent, and sustainable for you.
Is there a clean way to say I changed my mind without sounding flaky?
Yes: After thinking it through more carefully, I realized a different option makes more sense for me. That sounds deliberate rather than unreliable.
What is a good way to say no to a commitment if I am already stretched?
Be appreciative, brief, and clear. For example: Thank you for thinking of me. I cannot take this on and do it well with my current commitments, so I need to decline.
I want to ask if a course is too much for me, but I do not want to sound weak.
Frame it as a question about fit and balance, not ability alone. For example: I wanted your honest view on whether this course still looks like an appropriate fit within my overall schedule.
Can you summarize the difference between requirement and recommendation in one sentence?
A requirement is the minimum you need, while a recommendation is what is suggested for a stronger or more competitive path.
